title: Reactions and follow-up questions
description: Respond naturally to what people say â€” express surprise, sympathy, happiness â€” and ask the right questions to keep conversation going.

content.title: Reacting naturally to news
content.body:
Different types of news need different reactions:

GOOD NEWS:
â€¢ 'That's wonderful news!'
â€¢ 'Congratulations!'
â€¢ 'I'm so happy for you!'
â€¢ 'You must be thrilled!'
â€¢ 'Oh wow â€” that's brilliant!'

BAD NEWS:
â€¢ 'Oh no â€” I'm so sorry to hear that.'
â€¢ 'That's terrible â€” are you okay?'
â€¢ 'I can imagine how hard that must be.'
â€¢ 'Please let me know if there's anything I can do.'

SURPRISING NEWS:
â€¢ 'Really? I had no idea!'
â€¢ 'Seriously? I can't believe it!'
â€¢ 'You're joking! / No way!'
â€¢ 'That's incredible!'

INTERESTING NEWS:
â€¢ 'How fascinating!'
â€¢ 'That's really interesting â€” tell me more!'
â€¢ 'I didn't know that!'
content.grammar_note:
Reactions use exclamations and short phrases
'Must be' is used to empathise: 'You must be so excited!'
'I can imagine' shows you understand their feelings

content.title: Echoing and showing active listening
content.body:
ECHOING = repeating part of what someone said to show you're listening:

â€¢ 'I got the promotion!' â†’ 'The promotion! That's wonderful!'
â€¢ 'We moved to a new flat.' â†’ 'A new flat â€” how exciting!'
â€¢ 'I failed the exam.' â†’ 'Failed? Oh no â€” I'm so sorry!'

Other active listening techniques:
â€¢ Mm-hmm / Right / I see (shows you're following)
â€¢ 'Oh really?' (surprise + invitation to continue)
â€¢ 'And then what happened?' (asking for more detail)
â€¢ 'How did that make you feel?' (empathy)
â€¢ 'I bet!' (I imagine that!)
â€¢ 'That said a lot.' / 'That must have taken courage.'
content.grammar_note:
Echoing = repeat a KEY WORD with an exclamation
Active listening = show you're engaged, not just waiting to speak
'I bet!' = I can well imagine!

vocabulary.example: Oh, you must be gutted â€” I'm so sorry.

vocabulary.meaning: feeling glad that something bad didn't happen
vocabulary.example: I bet you're relieved it's over!
I was just completely overwhelmed by the news.

vocabulary.meaning: to understand and share someone's feelings
vocabulary.example: I really sympathise â€” that situation sounds so tough.

vocabulary.meaning: to truly understand how someone feels
vocabulary.example: I can empathise â€” I went through something similar.

sentences: Really? I had absolutely no idea â€” congratulations!
Oh no â€” that's terrible news. Are you doing okay?
You're joking! How did that happen?
I can imagine how hard that must have been for you.
That's brilliant â€” you must be absolutely thrilled!

content.question: Your colleague tells you she's just been offered a job at her dream company. What's the best reaction?
options: That's wonderful news â€” you must be over the moon! When do you start?
Make sure you negotiate the salary.
content.correct: That's wonderful news â€” you must be over the moon! When do you start?
content.explanation: Warm, genuine reaction ('wonderful news', 'over the moon') + natural follow-up question.

content.question: Your friend tells you her cat passed away. What's the most empathetic response?
options: Oh well, you can get another one.
Cats don't live that long anyway.
Oh no â€” I'm so sorry. She was such a lovely cat. How are you holding up?
content.correct: Oh no â€” I'm so sorry. She was such a lovely cat. How are you holding up?
content.explanation: Empathetic response: acknowledge the loss, show you care, and gently check on them.

content.question: Arjun tells you he climbed a mountain last weekend. What reaction keeps the conversation going best?
options: No way! Which mountain? How long did it take? Was the view worth it?
content.correct: No way! Which mountain? How long did it take? Was the view worth it?
content.explanation: Energetic reaction + multiple follow-up questions = shows genuine interest and keeps the conversation going.

content.question: What does 'You must be thrilled!' mean?
options: You have to be excited â€” I assume you're very happy.
content.correct: You have to be excited â€” I assume you're very happy.
content.explanation: 'Must be' = I assume this is how you feel based on the situation. 'Thrilled' = very excited and happy.

content.instruction: Write the best reaction + one follow-up question for each situation.

sentences.text: Friend: 'I'm getting married next March!' You: '___ ___ â€” ___?'
sentences.answer: Congratulations! / That's wonderful â€” who's the lucky person?

sentences.text: Colleague: 'I failed my driving test again.' You: '___ ___ â€” ___?'
sentences.answer: Oh no, I'm so sorry â€” was it the parallel parking again?

sentences.text: Friend: 'I ran my first 5K this morning!' You: '___ ___ â€” ___?'
sentences.answer: Oh wow, that's brilliant! â€” How long did it take you?

sentences.text: Colleague: 'My flight was cancelled and I had to sleep in the airport.' You: '___ ___ â€” ___?'
sentences.answer: Oh no, how awful! â€” How long were you stuck there?

content.instruction: Complete the reactions using the correct emotional word.

sentences.text: Friend passes a tough exam: 'You must be absolutely ___ !'
Friend's holiday was cancelled: 'Oh, you must be so ___ â€” I'm really sorry.'
Surprising news: 'You're ___ ! I had no idea that was happening!'
Empathising: 'I can ___ â€” that situation sounds incredibly difficult.'

content.instruction: Arrange into a warm reaction sentence.
Arrange into an empathetic bad news response.
content.answer: I can imagine how hard that must have been.

content.instruction: Match the news to the most natural reaction.

pairs.right: That's fantastic â€” you absolutely deserved it!
Oh no â€” that's awful! Did you manage to lock it remotely?
Really? That's such a big step! Are you excited or nervous?

pairs.left: 'I finished reading a 600-page novel in two days.'
pairs.right: You're joking! Two days? That's incredible!
Oh no â€” what a nightmare! What are you going to do now?

content.title: Catching up after a while â€” college friends, Bangalore coffee shop
content.setting: Priya (from Chennai) is visiting Bangalore and meets her old friend Kavya. They haven't spoken in months. The conversation is full of natural reactions and follow-up questions.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Priya! Oh my goodness â€” you look amazing. Sit, sit! How have you been?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I've been good! A bit all over the place honestly. So much has happened since we last talked!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Tell me everything! What's been going on?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Okay â€” so the big news: I resigned from my job three months ago.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What?! You resigned? I had no idea â€” are you okay? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm actually great! It was tough at first but the job was making me miserable for a long time.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I can imagine â€” you seemed quite stressed whenever we spoke last year. So what are you doing now?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm freelancing! I've been working with a few design clients online and it's going really well.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Oh wow â€” freelancing! How long did that take to set up? I always thought you'd be brilliant at it.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: It took about a month to get the first client, then it kind of snowballed. But the big thing is â€” I'm actually happy at work for the first time in years!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: That's such wonderful news, Priya â€” you must be so relieved! I'm genuinely happy for you.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I am! And what about you? You look well â€” anything new on your end?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Well... I got engaged last month!

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: No way! Kavya! Congratulations â€” I'm so happy for you! Who is he? Tell me everything!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ha! His name is Arjun â€” he works in Bangalore, we've been together for two years. It happened quite suddenly but it felt right.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: You must be absolutely over the moon! I want to see a photo right now!

comprehension_questions.question: What big change happened in Priya's work life?
How does Priya feel about her work now?
options: She is happy for the first time in years.
She wants to go back to employment.
comprehension_questions.correct: She is happy for the first time in years.

comprehension_questions.question: What is Kavya's big news?
options: She moved to a new city.

discussion_questions: Find 4 reaction phrases in the dialogue. What type of reaction is each one (surprise, happiness, sympathy)?
Find 3 follow-up questions. What purpose does each one serve?
What makes this conversation feel natural and warm? List 3 features.

content.instruction: These reactions are inappropriate or unnatural. Correct them.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'I got promoted!' You: 'I knew I was better than you for that role.'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'I got promoted!' You: 'That's amazing news â€” you really deserved it! Congratulations!'
sentences.explanation: Never make someone's good news about you or compare. Celebrate genuinely.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'My grandmother passed away.' You: 'Oh well, she was very old, wasn't she?'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'My grandmother passed away.' You: 'I'm so sorry to hear that â€” she sounded like such a wonderful person. Are you doing okay?'
sentences.explanation: Never minimise someone's grief. Show empathy and ask how they're coping.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'I'm moving abroad!' You: 'Don't you think that's a bit risky?'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'I'm moving abroad!' You: 'Wow â€” that's such an exciting step! Where are you going? You must be so excited!'
sentences.explanation: Don't question their decision with negativity â€” react positively and show interest.

content.scenario: Your colleague comes to work looking very upset. When you ask, she says she didn't get the project she applied for. She was really hoping for it. What do you say?
options: That's okay, there will be other projects.
Oh, I'm really sorry â€” you must be so disappointed. You worked so hard for that. Is there anything I can do?
content.correct: Oh, I'm really sorry â€” you must be so disappointed. You worked so hard for that. Is there anything I can do?
content.explanation: Acknowledge the feeling ('must be disappointed'), validate the effort ('worked so hard'), and offer support.

content.instruction: Respond to these 5 pieces of news with a natural reaction + follow-up question. Speak for at least 2 sentences per response.
content.prompt:
React to each piece of news:
1. 'I'm going to become a vegetarian!'
2. 'My flight was diverted to the wrong city.'
3. 'I finished learning Japanese in one year.'
4. 'My company offered me a transfer to London.'
5. 'I've decided to run a half marathon next month.'
content.example_response:
1. 'I'm going to become a vegetarian!' â€” Oh wow, that's a big change! What inspired the decision? Are you going full vegetarian or starting gradually? I think that's great â€” I've thought about it too sometimes.

2. 'My flight was diverted to the wrong city.' â€” Oh no, you're joking! That sounds like an absolute nightmare! Where did you end up? How long did it take to sort out? You must have been exhausted!

3. 'I finished learning Japanese in one year.' â€” Are you serious?! That's incredible â€” Japanese is supposed to be one of the hardest languages in the world! How did you do it? Were you taking formal classes?

4. 'My company offered me a transfer to London.' â€” Oh wow â€” that's huge! Are you excited or nervous? Have you decided whether you're going to accept? London is amazing but it's a big move!

5. 'I've decided to run a half marathon next month.' â€” Really?! That's so impressive â€” have you been training for a while? A half marathon is no joke â€” 21 kilometres! I bet you'll do brilliantly!

content.instruction: Share a piece of news from your own life â€” real or imagined â€” and describe how people reacted to it. Were their reactions good? What made a reaction feel warm or cold?
content.prompt: Tell a story about sharing some news with someone and how they reacted. What did they say? How did it make you feel?
content.example_response: I'd like to talk about when I told my family I'd decided to leave my stable engineering job to try freelancing. I was very nervous because I knew it was an unconventional choice. When I told my mother, her first reaction was quiet â€” then she said: 'Are you sure? What if it doesn't work out? You have a good job.' And honestly, even though she meant well, that reaction made me feel deflated. I'd expected more support. Then I told my best friend and she said: 'Oh my god â€” that's so brave! You've been wanting to do this for years. I'm so proud of you. What are your plans? How can I help?' And that reaction changed everything. I felt validated and excited. The same news got two completely different reactions â€” one focused on the risk, one on the possibility. I learned that when someone shares big news, the most important thing is to lead with warmth and interest, not with fear. Ask questions, show excitement, and save the practical concerns for later.

content.instruction: Complete this catching-up conversation with natural reactions.
content.text:
Kavya: I have some big news â€” I'm pregnant!

Priya: Oh ___ (1) â€” congratulations! You must be ___ (2)! When is the baby due?

Kavya: March! We're both over the moon.

Priya: ___ (3) â€” March is beautiful for a baby. How are you feeling?

Kavya: A bit tired but really happy. Oh â€” and my sister failed her board exams.

Priya: Oh ___ (4) â€” ___ (5) be so disappointed. Is she doing okay?

Kavya: She's upset but she's planning to retake them in six months.

Priya: That's the spirit â€” she'll do brilliantly next time! And your job â€” still loving it?

Kavya: Actually, I got a pay rise last month!

Priya: ___ (6)?! That's ___ (7) news â€” you completely ___ (8) it!

content.title: Lesson 9 complete â€” Reactions and follow-up questions
key_points: Good news: 'That's wonderful!', 'Congratulations!', 'You must be thrilled!'
Bad news: 'I'm so sorry', 'That sounds so tough', 'I can imagine'
Echoing = repeat a key word: 'A promotion? That's amazing!'
Active listening: Mm-hmm, Right, I see, I bet!
Follow-up questions: 'How longâ€¦?', 'Was it your first time?', 'How did that feel?'
Never minimise someone's news â€” always lead with warmth
